global_summary: The video discusses how the human prefrontal cortex allows us to simulate experiences, enabling us to make decisions based on imagined outcomes rather than just current circumstances. It explores how overestimating the difference between various life situations can lead to negative behaviors and emotions.

segments.transcript: When you have 21 minutes to speak, two million years seems like a really long time, but evolutionarily, two million years is nothing.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: Yet in two million years, the human brain has nearly tripled in mass, going from the one and a quarter pound brain of our ancestor here, Habalus, to the almost three pound meatloaf that everybody here has between their ears.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: What is it about a big brain that nature was so eager for everyone to have one?
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: Well, it turns out when brains triple in size, they don't just get three times bigger, they gain new structures.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: And one of the main reasons that our brain got so big is because it got a new part called the frontal lobe, and particularly a part called the prefrontal cortex.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: Now, what does a prefrontal cortex do for you that should justify the entire architectural overhaul of the human skull in the blink of evolutionary time?
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: Well, it turns out the prefrontal cortex does lots of things, but one of the most important things it does is an experience simulator.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: You know, pilots practice in flight simulators so that they don't make real mistakes in planes.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: Human beings have this marvelous adaptation that they can actually have experiences in their heads before they try them out in real life.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: This is a trick that none of our ancestors could do that no other animal can do quite like we can.
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: It's a marvelous adaptation. It's up there with opposable thumbs and standing upright in language as one of the things that...
segments.label: intro

segments.transcript: That got our species out of the trees and into the shopping mall.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Now, all of you have done this. I mean, you know, Ben and Jerry's doesn't have liver in on your ice cream, and it's not because they whipped some up, tried it and went yuck.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: It's because from without leaving your armchair, you can simulate that flavor and say yuck before you make it.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Let's see how your experience simulators are working. Let's just run a quick diagnostic before I proceed with the rest of the talk.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Here's two different futures that I invite you to contemplate, and you can try to simulate them and tell me which one you think you might prefer.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: One of them is winning the lottery. This is about $314 million, and the other is becoming paraplegic.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: So just give it a moment of thought. You probably don't feel like you need a moment of thought.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: And interestingly, there are data on these two groups of people, data on how happy they are. And this is exactly what you expected, isn't it?
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: But these aren't the data. I made these up. These are the data. You've failed the pop quiz in your hardly five minutes into the lecture.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Because the fact is that a year after losing the use of their legs, and a year after winning the lotto, lottery winners and paraplegics are equally happy with their lives.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Now, don't feel too bad about failing the first pop quiz because everybody fails all of the pop quizzes all of the time.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: The research that my laboratory has been doing, that economists and psychologists around the country have been doing, have revealed something really quite startling to us.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Something we call the impact bias, which is the tendency for the simulator to work badly.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: For the simulator to make you believe that different outcomes are more different than in fact they really are.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: From field studies to laboratory studies, we see that winning or losing an election, gaining or losing a romantic partner, getting or not getting a promotion, passing or not passing a college test on and on, have far less impact, less intensity and much less duration than people expect them to have.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: In fact, a recent study, this almost floors me, a recent study showing how major life traumas affect people suggests that if it happened over three months ago with only a few exceptions, it has no impact whatsoever on your happiness.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Why? Because happiness can be synthesized. Sir Thomas Brown wrote in 1642, I am the happiest man alive.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: I have that in me that can convert poverty to riches, adversity to prosperity. I am more invulnerable than a killee's fortune, half not one place to hit me.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Kind of remarkable machinery does this guy have in his head. Well, it turns out it's precisely the same remarkable machinery that all of us have.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Human beings have something that we might think of as a psychological immune system, a system of cognitive processes, largely non-conscious cognitive processes that help them change their views of the world so that they can feel better about the worlds in which they find themselves.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Like Sir Thomas, you have this machine unlike Sir Thomas, you seem not to know it.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: We synthesize happiness, but we think happiness is a thing to be found. Now, you don't need me to tell you, give you too many examples of people synthesizing happiness, I suspect though.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: I'm going to show you some experimental evidence. You don't have to look very far for evidence. I, as a challenge to myself, since I say this once in a while in lectures, I took a copy of The New York Times and tried to find some instances of people synthesizing happiness.
segments.label: content

I am so much better off physically, financially, mentally, and almost every other way. What other way would there be to be better off?

segments.transcript: We smirk because we believe that synthetic happiness is not of the same quality as what we might call natural happiness.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: What are these terms? Natural happiness is what we get when we get what we wanted, and synthetic happiness is what we make when we don't get what we wanted.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: In our society, we have a strong belief that synthetic happiness is of an inferior kind.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: Why do we have that belief? Well, it's very simple.

segments.transcript: What kind of economic engine would keep churning if we believe that not getting what we want could make us just as happy as getting it?
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: With all apologies to my friend Mathew Ricard, a shopping mall full of Zen monks is not going to be particularly profitable because they don't want stuff enough.

segments.transcript: I want to suggest to you that synthetic happiness is every bit as real and adoring as the kind of happiness you stumble upon when you get exactly what you were aiming for.

segments.transcript: Now I'm a scientist, so I'm going to do this not with a rhetoric, but by marinating you in a little bit of data.

segments.transcript: Let me first show you an experimental paradigm that is used to demonstrate the synthesis of happiness among regular old folks.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: This isn't mine, there's a 50-year-old paradigm called the free choice paradigm. It's very simple.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: You bring in say six objects, and you ask a subject to rank them from the most to the least like.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: In this case, because the experiment I'm going to tell you about uses them, these are Monet prints.

segments.transcript: So everybody can rank these Monet prints from the one they like the most to the one they like the least.

segments.transcript: Now we give you a choice. We happen to have some extra prints in the closet, and we're going to give you one as your prize to take home.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: We happen to have number three and number four. We tell the subject.
segments.label: content

segments.transcript: There's a bit of a difficult choice because neither one is preferred strongly to the other, but naturally people tend to pick number three because they like to the little better than number four.

segments.transcript: The great source of both the misery and disorders of human life seems to arise from overrating the difference between one permanent situation and another.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: Some of these situations may no doubt deserve to be preferred to others but none of them can be deserved, none of them can deserve to be pursued with that passionate order which drives us to violate the rules either of prudence or of justice or to corrupt the future tranquility of our society.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: We are not going to be able to do our minds either by shame from the remembrance of our own folly or by remorse for the horror of our own injustice.

segments.transcript: In other words yes, some things are better than others. We should have preferences that lead us into one future over another.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: But when those preferences drive us too hard and too fast because we have overrated the difference between these futures we are at risk.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: When our ambition is bounded it leads us to work joyfully. When our ambition is unbounded it leads us to lie, to cheat, to steal, to hurt others, to sacrifice things of real value.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: When our fears are bounded we are prudent, we are cautious, we are thoughtful when our fears are unbounded and overblown we are reckless and we are cowardly.
segments.label: outro

segments.transcript: The lesson I want to leave you with from these data is that our longings and our worries are both to some degree overblown because we have within us the capacity to manufacture the very commodity we are constantly chasing when we choose experience.
